title: Pick Your Fights
teaches: the fight-phase ordering decision: the fight you resolve first decides which enemy swings at full strength.
intro: Two brawls, one battlefield. On the left, your first mob of Boyz is locked with Necron Immortals — two attacks each, hitting on 3s: twenty dice waiting to fall on you. On the right, your second mob has pinned Necron Warriors — one attack each, hitting on 4s: a nuisance, not a threat. Here is the new part: when several combats share a fight phase, the players take turns choosing WHICH fight resolves next — and this opponent always picks its deadliest option. You choose first. Remember what First Blood taught you: dead models make no attacks. You will not wipe the Immortals — they are tough — but you do not need to. Every Immortal you drop before their turn is two dice they never roll. Which fight do you resolve first?

turns.narrate_before: Choppas up on both flanks. You pick the first fight — then the enemy picks, then you, then them, until every engaged unit has fought. Whichever enemy you leave waiting fights at whatever strength it still has when its moment comes.

outro: That was the ordering decision, and it is the whole art of the fight phase. Every engaged unit was always going to fight — you never chose WHETHER, only WHEN. But when is everything: the enemy you struck first answered with survivors, and the fight you kept waiting happened on your schedule, not theirs. Notice the opponent running the same arithmetic at you — every time it picked, it resolved its deadliest available fight first. Replay this scenario with the same seed and open with the OTHER fight: count the dice in the Immortals' ATTACKS line that time. The difference is what your first pick bought. When charges arrive in a later update, this decision sharpens further — charging units fight before everything else.
